speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Ralene Braesk. I'm conducting an interview with Bluma Doman. Today is the 18th of February, 1996. The interview is being conducted in Johannesburg, South Africa, in English, tape seven. You were telling me about your grandchildren in South Africa, and your years with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came over here, after my tragedy, my two grandchildren-- Didi was-- Chana was six, Bella was five, and Didi was three years old-- had just his [NON-ENGLISH]. I was very happy to see the children. And while I'm here, the other three got born, so that they grew up together with me. That's why they're very close to me. I love them with all my heart. It's just wonderful. Those kids, if I don't see them, you know, if they're busy with homework or other things, they always come to see baba.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didi-- no is [NON-ENGLISH], Pesse, and Mina, and Aaron-- his name, god bless him, he's eight years old, he's named after my husband, my late husband. In fact, my son doesn't call him ever by his name. He always calls him Tatala. And I called my son Tatala, because he is named after my father. And Bella is my mother's name. I never ask her by the name-- I always tell her mommy. Mama, you're my-- you're my mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you see your brother and your sister again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them two years ago, now in June. I left Los Angeles in 1980. And I came here. But we are in constant touch. We send-- we have even videos we're making. My son was in America. He brought home a beautiful video. He has the-- you know, the camera. So whenever he goes, he's taking with him. I made a beautiful video for them, the whole family. And my son took this to America. And he brought home my family in Los Angeles, a video. We are very, very well informed about each other. They-- know-- we call other, we sending fax-- we are very much in touch. And we love each other very much. After I've been-- I've been in Israel last time, my son made me a surprise. I got a letter from my sister that she's going to visit in-- in-- to Israel. They lived in Israel quite a number of years, you know, until they came to America. They couldn't come before we became citizens. We have no right to bring down-- when you're an American citizen, then you can bring down family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I got a letter from my sister. She come to visit. So my son said, mommy. I have no time now to read. When I-- after I close the office, I'll come to your room. He comes into my-- and sits down on the bed. I was in bed already. He said, mommy, I have a big surprise. I said, what? He said, if you write a letter to America, if Uncle Arieh and Auntie Miriam-- that's my brother-- if they go, and I'm sending you. I said, how can you send me? He said, what do you want? I'm sending you with Katherine-- with my maid, takes care of me six years. So, we're writing a fax. And my brother-in-law bought a fax machine, and special of that. And we got communicated, and-- which was-- was something. My son called-- no, got in touch with an agency in Poland, in Warsaw, which was making videos. This was the main thing which my son wanted me to go to Israel. So he got a notion. I always talk about, you know-- heads, about the family. But they know everything. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do I want to say? You were telling me about the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the tape. So he got in touch with the agency in Warsaw, a Polish agency. He wrote in a letter to them. And he said he wants to have a video of Ozorkow where my mother is born, and the house where she lived. I want to see everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right away, the agency calls my son back. They got good money for it. And they went a photographer, and this lady, they went to Ozorkow, and they start looking around.

speaker: interviewee
text: They found a goy, which lived across the street from my husband. And this goy describes the whole Ozorkow. The main thing is-- on the video is written, it's a documentary. In Polish, it's [POLISH]. If you were to understand Polish, what this guy was saying about our families, my husband was his friend. They lived across the street. They had a butcher shop. And my husband's parents had a grocery store. And how he speaks about my husband, about his brothers, they were a family of seven, about his sisters, all their names and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got my-- my son said to this lady, this video got to be made before June 14. Because my mother goes to Israel, and I want her to take that video. Was this in 1995?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's going to be now two years in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or in 1994?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And as I-- and he said to her, this-- so she send it by special mail, special delivery. He paid a fortune in dollars. They didn't want to-- they don't accept zlotys.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but-- the video was made so accurate, where we lived and how we lived. And he describes my father, how successful he was. What a tailor her was-- was the best in the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband's family, he describes the-- his-- I know, now I know who he is. His little sister was a friend with my husband's, also the youngest little sister. And he describes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got this. So when the video came, it was on a Friday. My son said to me, mommy, if you want to go to Israel, I want you a month in bed. You have to be rested, and that's it. So I went to bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, I wait. And they made my bed. I stayed in bed. And I was really very, very good in my health to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got the video. It was on a Friday. I was staying in bed. My son is coming in running, I got it! I got it! I got it! The video from Ozorkow is here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we saw the video. It was Friday, almost before Shabbat. He said, mommy, when-- tomorrow morning-- tomorrow night, we watch, and watch, and watch how many times you want. So then he made this video a copy. He didn't want me to take the original. And in the middle, we made the whole family-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] everybody had something to say. And at the end was the Lodz ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I took this to Israel. And I left it with my sister and brother. We watched it in the hotel in Israel. There were a lot of Ozorkow from our city, and they said, oh, who wants to spend so much money and so much time to make a video of Poland? So one guy said, he didn't do it for-- he did for his mother. If you don't like it, don't watch it. He did for his mother this, which was a very big thing. And the thousands of dollars my-- my son paid for this. Was it wonderful seeing your brother and sister again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was wonderful. The hello wasn't as bad, but the goodbye-- when we said goodbye, [NON-ENGLISH], I'll never forget it. Chani was there, too. Chani had just finished school. She waited for me to come to Israel. When we arrived, she had already the hotel accommodation. My brother was there, and my sister came a day later. And we had a wonderful reunion. But when we had to say goodbye, I'll never forget that. My brother fell on my shirt. He always felt very close to me, always. He felt to me-- maybe because I was the oldest. He fell on my shoulder. He didn't want to let me go. We cried-- my sister, my brother-in-law, Chani, and my sister-in-law. Finally, we broke and we went in the elevator and they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we are very close. I love them. They're very nice to me-- for every birthday, for everything I got the most beautiful clothes. When was the first time that you saw them after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, it was at least 10 or 14 years. We had been writing to each other. We're sending pictures. Meanwhile, my sister got married in Israel, and they decided to leave Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we made them papers. When they came to-- to Los Angeles, there was one cry. My brother was crying. He just couldn't break away from them. And he was married already, had one baby, has three sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a while, we brought down my brother. So we all lived in Los Angeles. Ironically, we lived all on the same street, numbers apart. All, we lived in Genesee, Genesee Avenue. Then, you know, everybody got busy. The children started growing up. There were bar mitzvahs, there were weddings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they also religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to extent, every Jew I've told you, Polish was religious. But not as much as we are. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you stand religiously today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love it. Nobody could take me away from it. Even which I-- which I went through in my life, I never turned away from religion, especially now. I'm living in such a religious environment. And you know, my grandchildren, my-- mostly Pesse-- she is so religious It's unbelievable. And sometimes she's watching me. You know, I-- you saw my place. I have a bathroom and that's it. My son didn't want to build me-- when I came here, they built it for me. Right away didn't, I-- they lived in the house, in one room. But my son said, no kitchen. You're in the chair-- I know you. You're going to burn yourself, no kitchen. I said, OK. So if I want to cook, I cook something which the children like. Because I cook a lot. They know when I do. When they're coming home from school, they open the cupboards. Oh, Baba cooked already something. They're coming home, I'm telling you, so hungry. And I feel bad lately, when I was so sick, I don't cook too much. And I feel bad. I said, Mina, mostly Mina-- I said, Mina, go eat something. No, I don't want to eat something. I want to eat what you cooked. Yeah. And Aaron, when we was little, he used to-- I got a little refrigerator. You saw it. He comes in, and he opens the refrigerator and I-- he said, Baba, you didn't cook today nothing. I said, look, there's halvah, there's other things. Go take it. No, you had to cook. It's not fair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you daven?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mostly go with my son to say them in shul. But I have lots of friends, and get together-- which I made it when I just came. I have nice friends over here, ladies, mostly widows, too. And I'm very liked. We have a club, zedas and babas. In fact, today was the meeting. I didn't go. When they called me for the meeting, Brenda told them I will not come today-- I'm busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the grandchildren say, Baba, you can go to meeting. It's 10 o'clock till 12:00. So my son said, no. Baba's going to be too tired. She's staying right here-- she don't go to the meeting. Does your son lead a congregation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He's just-- he has a wonderful, nice business, export/import. And he's making a very nice living, thank God. To raise [NON-ENGLISH] six children, sending to private schools, two daughters, well, in Israel, Chana, is going to be close to three years, is going to a seminar in New York. And it's-- it's a New York-- really, it's a New York school. But it's in Israel. You have to pay to fee in dollars. And so is Bella. Bella is now two years. She's coming after Pesse, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if the air fares and things, you have to have-- kind of have a lot. Two maids, we keep. They cost a lot of money, because one is just like a nurse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something is wrong with me. She's right there. They take me-- Brenda always tells them, if Baba needs you, she is first. You go. I have a wonderful daughter-in-law. I shouldn't say daughter-in-law. I should say daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about being interviewed today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel, I-- really I feel good. I wanted to tell the horror stories, which I lived through, and how it worked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: A message I can give to all Jewish people, don't turn away from your religion. Be a good Jew. Support your Israel. Give [NON-ENGLISH]. Give-- make mitzvahs. Because-- and hope to God that something like this will never happen. Is it OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wonderful family. They make me feel something special in this house. I'm very happy to be here, because no place in the world I could have a better home than I got here. My son is a rabbi. He's having different shuls. He's not having his own congregation. But he is in different shuls, mostly in Sydenham, Highlands North. This is my daughter-in-law. I should better say, she is my daughter. She is the one, schleps me to all the doctors. She is everything. When I get sick she is on top of everything more than my son. And now, I have my five grandchildren, one of my granddaughters is in Israel. This is Chani. And this is Pesse, and this is Mina, and this is Aaron, and this is Didi. And like I said, one is in Israel, which we miss very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You've just heard that Bella-- I'm the daughter-in-law, Brenda-- Bella, our second daughter is in Israel. And our fourth daughter, Pesse, has been voted to give a message from her, which came on the fax this morning. She, regretfully is not together with us for this wonderful occasion of filming. But her words convey very strong emotion. And when the children read her message, they all felt that it really said what they feel. So though it's written in the singular, it really is a message from all the children. Go ahead, Pesse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dear Baba, well, you know how very much I'd love to be there with all of you, and read this message myself, this will have to do. You've always been there for me in good times, and in the bad times, always with a smile, a listening ear, a warm word, and a helping hand. You've taught me, by example, to appreciate and to be happy for all the things that I have. You are a pillar of courage and strength. Thank you for everything you've done for me, for all the times that you've been there for me. Thank you very much for being the best grandmother. You know how much I love you. Love, Bella.

speaker: interviewee
text: This I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted also to say that we are all very grateful for this opportunity of being able to express our feelings, and for my mother-in-law to be able to tell her story, and place it on historical record as she has done for the past few hours. I feel that this entire project is monumental, and it has been a very important occasion in our home. From your daughter-in-law, we've learned so much from you, from your resilience. We've seen you up, we've seen you down. And you always come back very, very strong. We admire your great store of resources. There aren't many people made as strong as you, and we admire it. And we admire your amazing spirit. Our deep regret is that my father-in-law, Zedatudik, was not spared to enjoy these years with us, and to watch the grandchildren grow, and to have [NON-ENGLISH] and share with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, Mom, we look forward to the many years of gezunt with you, and happy times. And I think that's really what I need to say. And thank you for the opportunity. Chani?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to say that I'm very happy my grandma has the opportunity to tell her story. I'm very grateful. And we all love her very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being with my mother and having lived the stories with her throughout the years that you've heard in the past couple of hours, has been something that singles out a family, and singles out a parent like she is, and a father like my late father was, as well. It's taught all of us that difficult times are there to overcome. And when the chips are down, and everyone says there's no hope left in the world, there is hope. There's always been a Jewish tradition of hope, the hope of the Almighty. And it goes to show you that the most difficult things in life can be overcome. My mother, in very blunt terms, has had more [NON-ENGLISH] in one lifetime, than perhaps any other dozen people rolled in together. And throughout each one of those episodes in her life, she's come back strong, smiling, and with pride-- very important with pride. She's a lady who enjoys life. She's a lady who has a good priority of what-- a good recognition of the priorities of life. Having grown up as her son, has been for me a tremendous merit, something which is incomparable, indescribable. And to have the opportunity of having her here in my home and having my children be with her, to grow up with her together. Because is in a way, she's reliving a second childhood as well. Has been something that no one in the world could have given us, no one better than her. She's a tremendous mother. I love her dearly. She's a wonderful grandmother. She's one of the great cooks of this world. Her [NON-ENGLISH] and chicken soup are beyond anyone's comparison. And the fact that we have been here tonight, this evening, we give thanks to her, We give thanks to the Almighty, and thanks to all of us here-- to my children, and my wife especially, who has nursed her literally, through thick and through thin.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thanks are in order, then, to Mr. Spielberg for conceiving such a project. The idea is truly monumental, and incomparable in world history. And a special thanks as well to Mrs. Raylene Braesk, and to the videographer, Mike. Mike, what's your surname?

speaker: interviewer
text: Zeidel. Mike

speaker: interviewee
text: Mike Zeidel, who have shown during the past few hours, and the past few days in the pre-interviews as well, truly a great sensitivity and an insight into what this story is all about. We thank everyone. And we thank most of all, my dear mother. I've worn a badge of honor my whole life. That's a bit of lipstick on this cheek. Mom, give me the kiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: I kiss 10 times a day. Now, Didi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baba, it's not only a pleasure to have living with us, but also an honor. We all love you, Baba,

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Mina? Aaron?

speaker: interviewee
text: We love you, Baba.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mina? OK. Get up. Kiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me see your face. Come here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Can you sit here on mommy's lap? Nice, all right.
